change_summary: detail_evidence grew 92% (969→1862 chars) and detail_verdict_text grew 886% (98→966 chars) since c2 on 2026-04-07. Self-contradiction text was added per c2 minor_edit recommendation, and a templated post-hoc/trivial-transcription paragraph was added to detail_verdict_text. Re-review found one mechanical duplication bug and one templating misapplication.
our_argument_summary: Crepuscular rays are parallel sunbeams; apparent divergence is perspective. The decisive counter is anticrepuscular ray convergence at the anti-solar point — geometrically impossible with any local source. We add the dome model's own contradiction (05-model.txt labels 5,733 km an 'optical illusion due to aetheric refraction') and a verdict-level post-hoc structural critique invoking Section 3.1b's trivial-transcription test.

relevant_files: monitor.py domain 34 (per c1/c2 review — not in raw-text/, relying on prior code audit)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Static comparison. Per c1/c2: monitor.py hardcodes both predicted and observed as 5733; no API call, no triangulation math, no photo analysis. The dome's own AI-context file directs the model not to claim the sun physically occupies 5,733 km.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: 100% hardcoded on both sides per prior code audit. No live measurement. The 'check' is a self-equality.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome model's own documentation (05-model.txt, 07-ai-context.txt) labels 5,733 km as an optical illusion. Citing this as a self-contradiction is fair and is now incorporated.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None new. Prior c2 code finding (self-contradiction with 05-model.txt) is incorporated into detail_evidence.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Per c1/c2 audit, monitor.py domain 34 hardcodes 5733 on both sides. Current detail_evidence does not contradict this. The text presents perspective geometry as the standard-physics explanation rather than reporting any live measurement.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics explains crepuscular ray divergence as perspective on parallel rays from a distant source. The dome instead claims actual divergence from a local source at 5,733 km — a substantively different claim, not a vocabulary rename. The dome's concession that 5,733 km is illusory is internal contradiction, not relabeling.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Both predicted and observed are hardcoded to the same dome-internal parameter (5,733 km) in the same monitor.py file. There is no independent observation. Crepuscular ray photography long predates the dome model. Tag is correct, but the FRAMING in our verdict text is misleading (see hole below) — the right structural critique here is 'both sides hardcoded to the same parameter,' not 'value transcribed from undergraduate references.'
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: 5,733 km is a parameter input to the dome model (used in WIN-005 and elsewhere as a fixed sun altitude), not derived from dome geometry within WIN-026. Current tag (false) is correct.

holes_found.description: DUPLICATED PARAGRAPH in detail_evidence. The sentence 'Simple geometry: two shadow rays through cloud gaps separated by 5 km at 10 km altitude subtend a ~0.05° angular difference at ground level — a difference that only widens as the observer turns away from the sun. Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km.' appears VERBATIM TWICE in a row (positions 589 and 967 in the current detail_evidence string). This is a copy-paste/merge artifact rendered visibly to readers. A dome defender quoting our text could ridicule the editorial sloppiness; an AI 'debunk our debunk' query will see the duplication and may use it to dismiss the rigor of the review.
holes_found.recommendation: Delete the second occurrence of the duplicated sentence. Targeted edit removing characters ~967–1281 of detail_evidence (keep a single instance). After deletion detail_evidence should be ~1547 chars rather than 1862.

holes_found.description: MISAPPLIED TEMPLATE in detail_verdict_text. The text claims 'The numerical value is textbook physics (Part 3.1b, "trivial-transcription test"): it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources.' The numerical value at issue is 5,733 km — the dome's local-sun altitude. This value does NOT appear in any undergraduate textbook, NIST CODATA table, NASA fact sheet, IGRF dataset, USGS dataset, or Wikipedia infobox. Standard physics says the Sun is at ~149,597,871 km (1 AU); 5,733 km is a dome-specific input parameter. Section 3.1b's trivial-transcription test is defined for transcribed standard values like 'Schumann fundamental 7.83 Hz,' 'Earth equatorial radius 6,378 km,' or 'Moon orbital period 27.3 days.' WIN-026 fails the post-hoc test for a different reason — both predicted and observed are hardcoded to the same dome-internal parameter, with no live fetch — but the current verdict text invokes the wrong rule. A defender could legitimately respond 'you cited a test that doesn't apply, undermining your structural critique.'
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the trivial-transcription paragraph with a structural critique that fits WIN-026: 'This claim also fails our post-hoc test on structural grounds. monitor.py hardcodes both expected and observed values to the same dome-internal parameter (5,733 km), so the comparison is a self-equality with no independent measurement. Anticrepuscular convergence at the anti-solar point — the actual empirical content of the WIN — is never tested by the script; it cannot be, because the dome's own model documentation (05-model.txt) concedes the 5,733 km figure is an optical illusion rather than a physical altitude. There is no operational sense in which this is a prospective prediction of a real measurement. See Part 3.1b for the broader pattern of hardcoded-on-both-sides WINs.'

holes_found.description: The 0.05° angular calculation is quantitatively correct (5/(5733−10) = 0.0501°) but its rhetorical role is unclear. As written it sits between the anti-solar argument and the optical-illusion concession, but the calculation itself does not refute a 5,733 km sun — it shows the divergence is BELOW visual resolution at small distances (~9% of the Sun's 0.53° angular diameter). The calculation supports 'rays from a 5,733 km sun look approximately parallel at small horizontal scales,' which is essentially the dome's own perspective-illusion fallback. The decisive argument — anti-solar convergence over the full hemisphere — is qualitative, not quantitative. A careful reader can be left unsure which point is doing the work.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the connection. After the 0.05° figure, add: 'That 0.05° is ~9% of the Sun's angular diameter — small enough that a local 5,733 km sun would look near-parallel for short baselines, but converging rays at the anti-solar point require the rays to remain straight over the full sky hemisphere, which is geometrically incompatible with any finite source.' This makes explicit that the calculation handles the short-range objection while the qualitative anti-solar argument handles the decisive case.

holes_found.description: tldr_verdict says perspective geometry has been 'well understood since antiquity.' Mathematical perspective (linear vanishing-point projection) is a Renaissance development (Brunelleschi/Alberti, ~1413–1435), not antiquity. Ancient cultures observed parallel-line convergence intuitively but did not formalize it. Minor imprecision; does not affect the argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'since antiquity' with 'for centuries' or 'since the Renaissance.' Either is accurate without claiming more than the historical record supports.

stronger_arguments.description: Multi-station parallax test. If the sun were physically local at 5,733 km, two simultaneous photographs of crepuscular rays from observation points 100 km apart would show the rays diverging from physically different points on the sky (parallax of ~100/5733 rad ≈ 1.0° between the two observers' ray-origin points). At 1 AU the same parallax is 100/(1.5e8) rad ≈ 1.4e-4° — undetectable. Existing time-lapse plus multi-station crepuscular-ray photography does not show such parallax. Independent of the anti-solar argument and adds a quantitative falsification.
sources: Standard atmospheric optics: Minnaert 'The Nature of Light and Colour in the Open Air'; Lynch and Livingston 'Color and Light in Nature'

stronger_arguments.description: Sun angular diameter argument. The Sun is observed to subtend 0.53° from any point on Earth's surface. For a 5,733 km sun to appear 0.53° wide it must be ~50 km across (5,733 × tan(0.53°) ≈ 53 km). The dome model has not produced a derivation of why the local sun has exactly this physical size, and it does not predict variation in apparent size when observers are at different distances on the disc — but a 50 km object viewed from 5,733 km up vs ~9,000 km off-axis would change apparent diameter by ~30%, which is not observed. Parallel critique to ray-divergence and fits naturally on the same WIN.
sources: Apparent solar diameter measurements: 0.524–0.542° (perihelion/aphelion variation); standard astronomical references

kernel_of_truth.description: The visual impression of rays diverging from a point near the sun is genuine and intuitive — pre-perspective-theory cultures interpreted it as exactly that. The dome's claim is psychologically plausible; the model correctly identifies that a literal point-source would produce the visual pattern observers see. Mie/Rayleigh scattering as the visibility mechanism is sound physics.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The anti-solar test is the discriminator the dome cannot pass. Convergence at the point 180° from the Sun — including continuation to a vanishing point on the opposite horizon — requires that all rays remain mutually parallel (or near-parallel) over the full sky hemisphere. No finite-altitude source can produce this geometry. The dome's acknowledgment that 5,733 km is itself illusory makes the situation worse: the very altitude WIN-026 'confirms' is, by the model's own documentation, not the sun's physical position.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 3 review of WIN-026, triggered by change detection: detail_evidence grew 92% and detail_verdict_text grew 886% since c2 on 2026-04-07. The c2 minor_edit recommendation (incorporate the dome's self-contradiction on 5,733 km being labeled an optical illusion) HAS been applied — present in current detail_evidence. Two new issues introduced by post-c2 edits:

(1) A copy-paste duplication of the geometry sentence in detail_evidence — visible to readers, mechanical bug.

(2) Application of the trivial-transcription test template to a WIN where it does not fit. The trivial-transcription test (Section 3.1b) targets WINs whose 'predicted' values are textbook constants — Schumann 7.83 Hz, Earth radius 6,378 km, Moon orbital period 27.3 days. 5,733 km is NOT a textbook value; it is a dome-specific parameter. Cross-check: 17 WINs now reference the trivial-transcription test in detail_verdict_text (016, 017, 018, 019, 020, 021, 026, 029, 045, 046, 047, 049, 050, 051, 053, 055, 064). Most plausibly fit — Schumann, tidal periods, Hubble constant, IGRF/CHAOS-7 magnetic values. WIN-026 does not. The post-hoc CONCLUSION is correct (this is structurally post-hoc) but the REASON cited is wrong — the structural failure here is 'both sides hardcoded to the same dome-internal parameter,' not 'value transcribed from a textbook.'

The verdict (Refuted by Data) remains correct. The decisive empirical argument (anti-solar convergence) is intact. The c2 self-contradiction angle is incorporated. The two issues above are editorial / argumentative-precision issues, not verdict-affecting.

Cross-WIN consistency note: when WIN-026 invokes the trivial-transcription test for 5,733 km it is implicitly claiming that value is textbook physics. Elsewhere in our review (Section 1.8 Refraction Cascade and our citation of 05-model.txt) we treat 5,733 km as a contested dome parameter the model itself walks back. These two framings sit uneasily together and a careful reviewer could call out the mismatch.

citations_verified: https://atoptics.co.uk/blog/anticrepuscular-rays/ — atmospheric-optics enthusiast site; content accurate; live link
raw-text/05-model.txt — quote '(optical illusion due to aetheric refraction)' verified at line 38-39 per c2 review
citations_unchecked: https://john09289.github.io/predictions — author definition link inline in detail_verdict_text; not re-verified this cycle.
Internal anchor #posthoc-standard — should resolve to Part 3.1b in rendered HTML; anchor target verification belongs to Integrity rather than Curmudgeon.

advocate_mode.best_defense: WIN-026 does not require 5,733 km to be the physical sun altitude. The WIN claims only that crepuscular rays VISIBLY diverge from a near-zenith source, consistent with a local source ANYWHERE in the dome. The 5,733 km figure is the model's nominal observed altitude (acknowledged as an optical/refractive position, not a physical one). Anticrepuscular 'convergence' is itself a perspective effect — the rays only APPEAR to converge at the anti-solar vanishing point exactly as railroad tracks appear to converge in both directions. A photographer's eye is interpreting the same parallel-perspective geometry the globe model invokes; the dome model just relocates the source from infinity to the firmament ceiling. As long as the actual rays inside the dome are reasonably collimated by the firmament cavity (or by aetheric optics), the same visual pattern appears.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add one sentence to detail_evidence after the anti-solar argument: 'A local-source defender may claim the anti-solar convergence is itself perspective, not literal convergence. But perspective convergence at TWO opposite vanishing points (the Sun direction AND the anti-solar direction) requires the underlying rays to be parallel over the whole sky — which a finite-altitude source cannot produce, regardless of any optical/refractive caveat about that altitude.' This preempts the strongest dome-defender response by closing the 'collimating aether' / 'perspective cuts both ways' loophole.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-026's detail_verdict_text invokes the trivial-transcription test on the assumption that 5,733 km is a textbook value. Section 1.8 (Refraction Cascade) and our citations of 05-model.txt treat 5,733 km as a contested dome parameter that the model itself describes as an optical illusion. These framings within the same review are not directly contradictory but they pull in opposite rhetorical directions — 'too well-known to count as a prediction' vs 'dome's own internal contradiction.' The 'both sides hardcoded' structural argument resolves the tension and is the right framing here.

claims_checked: 0.05° angular difference for two cloud gaps 5 km apart at 10 km altitude with sun at 5,733 km
Sun at 150M km gives effectively parallel rays for the same geometry
quantitative_verification.computations: 5 / (5733 − 10) rad × (180/π) = 0.0501° ✓ matches the text's '~0.05°.' For sun at 150,000,000 km, same gap geometry gives 1.91e-6° — effectively zero. Sun's observed angular diameter ranges 0.524–0.542° depending on Earth-Sun distance; '~0.53°' is correct. Ratio 0.0501/0.53 ≈ 0.095, so 0.05° is ~9% of the Sun's angular width. Multi-station parallax check (added under stronger_arguments): 100 km baseline at 5733 km altitude = 100/5733 rad = 0.999° — on order of a degree; same baseline at 1 AU = 1.4e-4° — undetectable. Consistent with the stronger_arguments addendum.
